questions.questionText: Histamine H1 receptor antagonists (e.g., chlorpheniramine, cetirizine) are used for:
questions.optionB: Allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and allergic conjunctivitis (block H1-mediated effects)
questions.explanation: H1-antihistamines: block H1 receptors → reduce itching, sneezing, rhinorrhea, urticaria, allergic conjunctivitis. First-generation (sedating): chlorpheniramine, diphenhydramine, promethazine — cross BBB → sedation, anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation). Second-generation (non-sedating): cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine — do NOT significantly cross BBB. H2 blockers (ranitidine, famotidine): gastric acid suppression. Reference: KD Tripathi — Antihistamines.

questions.questionText: NSAIDs cause GI adverse effects because they:
questions.optionB: Inhibit COX-1 → decrease protective prostaglandins (PGE2, PGI2) → reduced mucus/bicarbonate secretion and mucosal blood flow
questions.explanation: NSAIDs inhibit COX-1 (constitutive) → decreased GI protective prostaglandins (PGE2 maintains mucus/bicarbonate secretion, mucosal blood flow) → gastric erosions, ulcers, GI bleeding. COX-2 selective inhibitors (celecoxib): less GI toxicity but cardiovascular risk (prothrombotic — decreased PGI2). Prevention of NSAID gastropathy: PPI (omeprazole) or misoprostol (PGE1 analog). Aspirin: irreversible COX inhibition → prolonged GI risk + antiplatelet effect. Reference: KD Tripathi — NSAIDs GI Effects.

questions.questionText: Montelukast is a leukotriene receptor antagonist used for:
questions.optionB: Prophylaxis of bronchial asthma (especially exercise-induced and aspirin-induced asthma)
questions.explanation: Montelukast: oral cysteinyl leukotriene receptor (CysLT1) antagonist → blocks LTC4/LTD4/LTE4 → reduces bronchoconstriction, mucus secretion, edema, eosinophil recruitment. Uses: prophylaxis of mild-moderate asthma (add-on to ICS), exercise-induced bronchospasm, aspirin-induced asthma (leukotrienes are major mediators when COX is blocked). NOT for acute rescue. Zileuton: 5-LOX inhibitor (blocks leukotriene synthesis) — less commonly used due to hepatotoxicity. Reference: KD Tripathi — LT Antagonists.

questions.questionText: 5-HT3 receptor antagonists (ondansetron, granisetron) are used primarily as:
questions.explanation: 5-HT3 antagonists: block serotonin receptors in CTZ (chemoreceptor trigger zone) and vagal afferents → potent antiemetic effect. Most effective for chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting (CINV — especially with cisplatin), post-operative nausea/vomiting (PONV), radiation-induced emesis. Ondansetron: prototype. Side effects: headache, constipation, QT prolongation. Often combined with dexamethasone for enhanced antiemetic effect. NK1 receptor antagonist (aprepitant): additional antiemetic for highly emetogenic chemo. Reference: KD Tripathi — 5-HT3 Antagonists.

questions.questionText: Prostaglandin E1 analog misoprostol is used for:
questions.optionB: Prevention of NSAID-induced gastric ulcers and medical termination of pregnancy (with mifepristone)
questions.explanation: Misoprostol: synthetic PGE1 analog. Gastric effects: increases mucus/bicarbonate secretion, increases mucosal blood flow, decreases acid secretion → prevents NSAID-induced gastropathy. Uterine effects: stimulates uterine contractions (causes cervical ripening) → used for medical abortion (with mifepristone — RU-486), induction of labor, post-partum hemorrhage. Contraindicated in pregnancy (abortifacient). Side effects: diarrhea (most common), abdominal cramps. Reference: KD Tripathi — Misoprostol.
